preFix.title: Injecting a chemical damp-proof course
preFix.subtitle: Install a silicone DPC by injection into the mortar course to stop rising damp
preFix.excerpt: How to inject a silicone-based chemical damp-proof course into the mortar joint at the base of an internal masonry wall to stop rising damp, covering drilling the injection holes, filling them with cream, and re-pointing.
preFix.sourceNotes: British Standard BS 6576: code of practice for installation of chemical DPCs. BRE Good Repair Guide on rising damp. Property Care Association guidance on chemical DPC systems. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: A
chemical DPC
is the domestic remedy for
rising damp

in walls where the original physical DPC has failed or does not exist. The silicone cream is injected into the mortar joint at 150 mm below the floor level or at the lowest accessible mortar joint. Once injected, it spreads through the mortar and brick by diffusion and sets to form a water-repellent band. The injection is the straightforward part; the re-plastering that follows is where most of the time goes.
What you will need: materials

Silicone DPC cream in a cartridge (Dryzone, Wykamol, or any product listing BS 6576 compliance). A cream gun or standard cartridge gun. A 12 mm masonry drill bit and an extension if needed for the drill depth. Mortar for re-pointing the injection holes. Renovation plaster (salt-resistant) for re-plastering the damp-affected area after the DPC has set and the wall has had time to dry.
Step 1, confirm rising damp before drilling
Confirm the problem is rising damp before committing to injection. Rising damp shows a horizontal band of damp at low level, with
efflorescence

and tide marks. Penetrating damp from a crack or failed pointing tends to be at a higher level and follows rain; condensation is at cool surfaces and appears in winter. Using a damp meter confirms moisture in the wall but does not distinguish the source: the pattern and position of the damp is the primary diagnostic. A damp specialist survey is worthwhile for a significant problem.
Step 2, drill the injection holes

Drill 12 mm holes into the mortar joint at 120 mm centres along the course that will carry the DPC, typically the mortar joint immediately above the floor level or at 150 mm above outside ground level. Drill to a depth of two-thirds of the wall thickness: do not drill all the way through. On a 225 mm solid brick wall, drill to 150 mm. Angle the holes slightly downward (about 5 degrees) so the cream does not run out when injected. Blow the dust from each hole with a puff of air before injecting.
Step 3, inject the cream

Load the cream into a cartridge gun or cream injection gun. Insert the nozzle to the back of the hole and inject as you slowly withdraw, filling the hole completely from back to front. The cream should emerge slightly at the hole mouth when the hole is full: this confirms the back of the hole is filled. Move to the next hole and continue across the wall. Fill holes in one pass along the whole wall; do not leave unfilled holes for more than one to two hours, as the cream may begin to set.
Step 4, re-point the injection holes

Allow the cream to penetrate and set for the time specified on the product (typically 24 hours). Then fill the injection holes with mortar, raked back to match the profile of the existing mortar joints. A straight-struck mortar joint uses the filling knife to draw the mortar flush with the brick face. Leave the re-pointing to set fully before any replastering work above.
Step 5, allow the wall to dry and replaster

The DPC injection itself takes one day but the wall takes weeks to months to dry out before replastering. Remove the affected plaster to 500 mm above the tide mark, or to 1 m height as a general rule. Allow the wall to dry for at least three months in a warm, ventilated room. Use a renovation plaster (not standard multi-finish) when replastering: renovation plaster is salt-resistant and will not blow off as the salts work out through the new plaster.
Variations

Cavity walls are more complex. The outer leaf must be injected; if the cavity contains blown-in insulation, that insulation may be carrying moisture across to the inner leaf and will need to be addressed separately. A cavity DPC injection covers the outer leaf only. A continuous horizontal crack in the wall (the original physical DPC failing as a structural element) needs structural advice before injection is used on its own.
Care

A correctly injected chemical DPC lasts 20 to 30 years. Maintain ground levels outside the wall below the DPC level: if soil or paving piled against the wall rises above the DPC, the rising damp will bypass it. Check the pointing of the mortar joints above the DPC annually: any crack above the DPC that allows water in from outside can appear as rising damp inside.

attrs.fix: Allow three to six months after injection before drawing conclusions about its effectiveness. If the damp continues at the same level after six months with no change in pattern, revisit the diagnosis: penetrating damp or a failed lead flashing above is the more likely cause.
attrs.cause: The DPC has not yet set; or the problem was not rising damp but penetrating or condensation damp.
attrs.symptom: Damp continues above the DPC injection level after treatment

attrs.fix: Remove the plaster, allow the wall to dry for a further one to two months, and replaster with a salt-resistant renovation plaster. Never use standard plaster over a treated rising-damp wall.
attrs.cause: Standard multi-finish plaster used over a wall that still contains salts; the salts crystallise behind the plaster and push it off.
attrs.symptom: New plaster blows or lifts off the wall within weeks of application

attrs.fix: Re-drill to the correct depth and inject a second pass. In very soft or open mortar, the cream dissipates faster than expected; fill with slightly more volume.
attrs.cause: The drill depth was insufficient; or a void or soft mortar section allowed the cream to flow away before filling the hole properly.
attrs.symptom: Cream did not fill the full depth of some holes on injection
